feedback.text: As a life-long consumer of video games, the lack of regulation of video game products is of great concern to me. With the increased popularity of digital storefronts has come the erosion of consumer rights. Many consumers, myself included, spend hundreds or thousands of euros on game products only to have the product we paid for taken from us due to a lack of end of life support. I spent much of my time playing Blizzard's Overwatch, in total I spent several hundred euros on the game both on access and additional purchases. Blizzard failed to provide end of life support and I along with many others lost access to a product we paid for. Of course many others have spent more money than I ever did on the game in question, and probably felt the loss much more severely than I. I've known many consumers who spend sums in the thousands to gain access to video games and additional content. Many massively multiplayer online games (MMOs) release expansions on top of the initial access purchase which range from 30 euros to 70 euros. Guild Wars 2, a game I personally play and have spent hundreds of euros on expansions for, utilizes such a model. In my opinion it is the game company's responsibility to provide adequate end of life support so consumers may retain access to products they paid for. Private servers for online games are already a common occurrence, with fans coming together to maintain beloved titles after official support ends, so what's stopping game companies from putting in that slight bit of effort to provide an official and easily accessible private server option? The lack of regulation mainly, or so it seems to me at least. As it stands currently, without proper regulation, video games serve as an avenue for corporations to collect profit in exchange for a product which can at a moment's notice be withdrawn without a valid reason. Many live service titles utilize predatory monetization practices that the EU has already addressed (such as the use of fake currencies to distort the pricing of in-game content) and I would like to use my feedback here to voice my thoughts on this matter as well. While marketing is a necessary part of selling a product, the excessive manipulation behind many video game storefronts is all too predatory. Without regulation consumer rights will progressively erode and most likely extend outside of the games industry. Video games are simply the first step toward corporations slowly taking away our rights to make informed purchases and own what we pay for. Video games are a medium enjoyed by all age groups and people from all walks of life. Many vulnerable groups find enjoyment and escape in the medium, and in the case of online games (in which consumer rights are the most violated) community among like-minded peers. I'm not just talking about children, but people who have become isolated or otherwise disenfranchised due to disability or life crisis. I have personally heard many stories from those struggling with gambling addiction moving onto video games as a distraction from their destructive habit, only to end up in another loop of destruction thanks to gambling-like monetization strategies (''gacha'' and loot boxes). Dark psychology is used in research to craft manipulative storefronts with little to no friction leading to purchase and exaggerated reward for making that step. The utilization of flashing lights, confusing workaround currencies, exciting sound effects, drawn-out animation sequences leading up to the final reveal and so on, all mimic the design of slot machines specifically designed to encourage a flow-state and keep consumers purchasing more. Thank you for lending your time and attention to consider both my personal feedback and the rights of video game consumers at large.
